seo.title: Legal Agentic RAG AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for agentic rag AI agents in legal. Covers Attorney-client privilege protections, Bar association ethics rules, Court filing standards.

content.intro: Deploying agentic RAG in legal practice carries existential risk: a single hallucinated case citation can trigger malpractice liability, bar sanctions, and case dismissal, as demonstrated by the Mata v. Avianca precedent where AI-fabricated citations resulted in court sanctions. Unlike passive AI tools, multi-step retrieval agents autonomously plan queries and synthesize across sources, multiplying the surface area for compounding errors before any attorney reviews the output. This checklist operationalizes the controls needed to deploy legal RAG agents responsibly, covering retrieval integrity, citation verification, privilege protection, and mandatory human oversight.

sections.heading: Citation Integrity & Hallucination Prevention
sections.description: Legal RAG agents must produce only verifiable, real citations. Hallucinated case law is the single highest-consequence failure mode in this domain, with direct precedent for court sanctions and malpractice exposure.

items.label: Implement citation grounding verification
items.description: Every case citation generated must be cross-referenced against a verified legal database (Westlaw, LexisNexis, or CourtListener) before inclusion in any output. The agent must not synthesize citations from partial matches or semantic similarity alone.

items.label: Deploy a dedicated citation validation step in the agent pipeline
items.description: Add an explicit post-synthesis pipeline stage that programmatically queries the citation database API for each generated citation, confirms the case name, docket number, court, and year, and flags any citation that cannot be confirmed as a hard error requiring human review.

items.label: Enforce source-faithful generation with attribution tokens
items.description: Configure the LLM to generate inline source attribution for every factual or legal claim, linking each statement to the specific retrieved chunk and page. Outputs without traceable attribution must be blocked from delivery.

items.label: Set hallucination detection thresholds using faithfulness scoring
items.description: Integrate a faithfulness evaluation model (e.g., RAGAS, TruLens, or custom NLI classifier) that scores each generated sentence against its retrieved source. Flag outputs below a 0.85 faithfulness threshold for mandatory attorney review before use.

items.label: Prohibit citation synthesis from secondary sources
items.description: Configure retrieval agent rules to prevent generating citations derived only from law review articles, legal blogs, or secondary commentary. All citations must trace back to primary source documents in the retrieval corpus.

items.label: Log all generated citations with retrieval provenance
items.description: Maintain an immutable audit log of every citation produced, including the source document, chunk ID, retrieval timestamp, and the specific query that triggered retrieval, to support post-hoc verification and malpractice defense.

items.label: Validate that cited cases have not been overruled or negatively treated
items.description: Integrate a Shepard's Citations or KeyCite API call for every case citation to confirm current precedential status. Overruled or distinguished cases must be flagged with their negative treatment history before attorney review.

sections.heading: Attorney-Client Privilege & Data Confidentiality
sections.description: Agentic RAG systems that retrieve across multiple sources and maintain session context create novel privilege waiver risks. Client data must be isolated, never used for training, and protected from cross-matter contamination.

items.label: Implement strict matter-level data isolation in the retrieval index
items.description: Partition the vector store and document corpus by client matter ID with access control enforcement at query time. The retrieval agent must be architecturally prevented from retrieving documents across matter boundaries without explicit attorney authorization.

items.label: Contractually prohibit LLM provider from using client data for model training
items.description: Obtain a signed Data Processing Agreement (DPA) from every LLM and embedding model provider explicitly prohibiting use of any query, document, or output containing client information for model training, fine-tuning, or evaluation.

items.label: Classify and tag all ingested documents for privilege status before indexing
items.description: Run a privilege classification step on every document before it enters the retrieval corpus. Documents tagged as privileged must be stored in a separate, access-controlled index with enhanced audit logging on every retrieval event.

items.label: Disable agent memory persistence across client sessions
items.description: Configure the agentic system to clear all session context, retrieved chunks, and intermediate reasoning steps at the end of each matter session. No client-specific information should persist in agent memory or cache between sessions.

items.label: Implement third-party disclosure detection in query planning
items.description: Add a pre-retrieval filter that detects when the agent's query plan would route client-confidential information to external APIs or third-party retrieval sources, and block or alert before execution.

items.label: Conduct privilege waiver risk assessment for multi-source retrieval
items.description: Before deployment, have supervising attorneys review the agent's query planning logic to identify scenarios where retrieving across public and private sources in a single session could constitute inadvertent privilege waiver under applicable jurisdiction rules.

items.label: Encrypt all client documents at rest and in transit within the retrieval pipeline
items.description: Apply AES-256 encryption to all stored documents and embeddings, and enforce TLS 1.3 for all data in transit between retrieval components. Encryption keys must be managed per-client, not shared across the system.

sections.heading: Mandatory Attorney Oversight & Review Workflow
sections.description: Bar association ethics rules and AI-generated content regulations require licensed attorney review of all AI-produced legal work product. The agent architecture must enforce human-in-the-loop checkpoints, not treat them as optional.

items.label: Enforce mandatory attorney review gate before any output is filed or sent
items.description: Implement a hard workflow gate that requires a licensed attorney to explicitly approve any agent-generated output before it can be used in a court filing, client communication, or legal document. The system must make it technically impossible to bypass this step.

items.label: Display AI-generated content warnings on all agent outputs
items.description: Every output produced by the agent must display a persistent, non-dismissible banner identifying it as AI-generated content requiring attorney review, including the agent version, retrieval sources used, and timestamp of generation.

items.label: Require attorney attestation log for each reviewed output
items.description: Create an immutable record of attorney review that captures the reviewing attorney's bar number, the specific output reviewed, the date and time of review, and whether the output was approved, modified, or rejected.

items.label: Define and document the agent's authorized scope of autonomous action
items.description: Produce a written policy specifying exactly which tasks the agent may complete autonomously (e.g., internal research summaries) versus which require attorney approval at each step (e.g., drafting court filings, advising on strategy). This policy must be reviewed by ethics counsel.

items.label: Implement confidence scoring thresholds that escalate to attorney review
items.description: Configure the agent to automatically escalate to attorney review when its internal confidence score falls below defined thresholds, when cross-source consistency checks fail, or when the query involves novel legal questions not well-represented in the retrieval corpus.

items.label: Train all attorneys using the system on AI supervision obligations
items.description: Deliver documented training to every attorney who will supervise or use agent outputs, covering their ethical duty of competence with AI tools under ABA Model Rule 1.1, the specific failure modes of this agent, and the firm's review procedures.

items.label: Establish an incident response protocol for agent errors discovered post-use
items.description: Define a written procedure for what attorneys must do if a hallucinated citation or erroneous legal analysis from the agent is discovered after a document has been filed or sent, including client notification obligations and bar reporting requirements.

sections.heading: Retrieval Architecture & Query Planning Controls
sections.description: Multi-step agentic query planning introduces compounding error risk: a flawed initial query plan propagates through all downstream retrieval and synthesis steps. The architecture must include validation at each planning and retrieval stage.

items.label: Validate query plan legal relevance before execution
items.description: Before the agent executes a multi-step retrieval plan, run the plan through a legal relevance classifier that confirms each planned query maps to a recognized legal research task. Reject or flag plans that include queries outside the defined scope of the matter.

items.label: Implement cross-source consistency checking after multi-source retrieval
items.description: After retrieving from multiple sources (e.g., case law database, statutory corpus, internal documents), run an automated consistency check that identifies contradictions between retrieved passages before synthesis. Flag contradictions for attorney resolution rather than allowing the agent to silently resolve them.

items.label: Restrict retrieval sources to a pre-approved, jurisdiction-verified corpus
items.description: Maintain an allowlist of approved retrieval sources for each jurisdiction. The agent must be prevented from retrieving from unapproved sources, and the allowlist must be reviewed quarterly by a supervising attorney to confirm sources remain current and authoritative.

items.label: Log and audit every step of the agent's query planning chain
items.description: Capture a complete, immutable trace of the agent's reasoning at each planning step, including the initial query decomposition, each sub-query issued, the sources queried, the chunks retrieved, and the synthesis decisions made. This trace must be retained for the duration of the matter plus applicable statute of limitations.

items.label: Set maximum retrieval depth and source count limits per query session
items.description: Define hard limits on the number of retrieval hops, sources queried, and documents retrieved per agent session to prevent runaway retrieval that exceeds attorney review capacity. Recommended starting limits: 5 retrieval steps, 3 external sources, 50 document chunks per session.

items.label: Test query planning accuracy against a labeled legal research benchmark before deployment
items.description: Before production deployment, evaluate the agent's query planning accuracy on a benchmark of at least 100 labeled legal research tasks representative of the firm's practice areas. Document precision, recall, and failure modes, and establish a minimum acceptable accuracy threshold.

items.label: Implement jurisdiction-aware retrieval routing
items.description: Configure the retrieval agent to automatically route queries to jurisdiction-specific corpora based on the matter's governing law. Prevent the agent from retrieving case law from non-controlling jurisdictions without explicitly labeling it as persuasive authority only.

items.label: Monitor retrieval latency and alert on degraded source availability
items.description: Instrument the retrieval pipeline to detect when primary legal databases are unavailable or returning degraded results. Alert the supervising attorney immediately rather than allowing the agent to fall back to lower-quality sources without disclosure.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: ABA Model Rule 1.1 (Competence) requires attorneys to understand the benefits and risks of relevant technology, including AI agents. Firms must document attorney training on the specific RAG agent's capabilities and failure modes, and supervising attorneys bear personal ethical responsibility for all AI-generated work product submitted under their name.

The Mata v. Avianca (S.D.N.Y. 2023) sanctions order established that attorneys cannot rely on AI-generated citations without independent verification. Firms must implement technical controls—not just policy—that make citation verification a mandatory, logged step before any AI-generated legal research is used in filings or client advice.

Client confidentiality under ABA Model Rule 1.6 and state equivalents extends to all information processed by the RAG agent, including query logs, retrieved chunks, and intermediate reasoning traces. Any vendor agreement for LLM or retrieval services must include explicit prohibitions on data use for training and must be reviewed by ethics counsel before execution.

eDiscovery obligations under FRCP Rule 26 and the Sedona Principles require that all AI-assisted document review and legal research be defensible and auditable. The agent's complete retrieval and reasoning logs must be preserved as potentially discoverable work product, and the firm must be prepared to disclose AI tool usage to opposing counsel and courts when required by local rules.

cta.heading: Automate Your Legal Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your legal RAG agent against this checklist, automatically flag citation verification failures and privilege risks, and generate the attorney review audit trails your firm needs to deploy AI confidently and ethically.
